questions.question: What does the `sizes` attribute on an `` tag describe?
choices: The intrinsic pixel width of each candidate file
The rendered width of the image in CSS pixels at various viewport breakpoints
The total disk size of each candidate file in bytes
The display density (DPR) for each candidate
questions.explanation: `sizes` tells the browser how wide the image will *display* (in CSS pixels) at various viewports. Combined with `srcset` (intrinsic widths) and the device pixel ratio, the browser picks the smallest candidate that still hits the target resolution.

questions.question: Your post has a hero image at the top of the page and three screenshots scattered through the body. Where should `loading="lazy"` go?
choices: On every image, including the hero - it's strictly better than not having it
On the three screenshots; omit it from the hero so it doesn't delay Largest Contentful Paint
Only on the hero, since it's the largest
On none of them - `loading="lazy"` is deprecated
questions.explanation: `loading="lazy"` on a below-the-fold image is a free win. On an above-the-fold hero, lazy-loading delays the LCP fetch and harms Core Web Vitals. The rule of thumb: lazy on everything below the fold, eager on the hero.

questions.question: Your blog publishes posts with one hero image each. Should you reach for the `jekyll-picture-tag` plugin or hand-roll the responsive `` markup?
choices: Always use the plugin - hand-rolled markup is incorrect for responsive images
Manual is simpler and adequate at this volume; the plugin pays for itself when you regularly include several images per post
Hand-roll always - plugins break GitHub Pages builds entirely
Use neither; modern browsers handle responsive image selection automatically without `srcset`
questions.explanation: The plugin's value scales with image count - it spares you handwriting many `<picture>` blocks but adds build-time and a libvips/ImageMagick dependency. For one hero per post, a small `_includes/figure.html` and three manual exports is less infrastructure for the same result.
